item.title: Enable 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) — TOTP & Email OTP with Docker
item.author: Worapon Asavanik
item.content:encoded: Enable 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) — TOTP & Email OTP with DockerSet up 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) running in Docker. This guide covers both TOTP (Google/Microsoft Authenticator) and Email OTP via Gmail SMTP.In this guide, we’ll enable two types of 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode):, which can be used immediately by scanning a QR code with Google or Microsoft Authenticator, and , which requires additional setup.For Email OTP, we’ll configure a and modify both config.py and config_local.py to enable and customize multi-factor authentication.While config.py contains the main settings, config_local.py is used to safely override specific configurations without modifying the core system file — which is useful in containerized environments.Example configurations include enabling MFA, customizing the email subject, and setting up SMTP credentials such as:MFA_ENABLED = TrueMFA_FORCE_REGISTRATION = FalseMAIL_SERVER = 'smtp.gmail.com'MAIL_PORT = 465MAIL_USERNAME = 'your_email@gmail.com'MAIL_PASSWORD = 'your_app_password'This article covers:1.Quick overview of 2FA — What it is and why it’s important for pgAdmin security3.TOTP: Enable TOTP authentication — Set up login via authenticator apps (no extra config needed)Prepare Gmail SMTP credentials (App Password) for pgAdmin — Make sure pgAdmin has 2FA support enabled— Set email-related settings like SMTP credentials7.Summary of All CommandsTwo-Factor Authentication (2FA) adds an extra layer of security to your pgAdmin login by requiring a second form of verification, typically beyond just a password.3.TOTP: Enable TOTP authenticationStart by accessing the pgAdmin 4 server via http://<host_ip>:<pgadmin_port> in your browser. Then, log in using the username and password you defined in the docker-compose.yml file.After accessing the pgAdmin dashboard, look at the top-right corner and click on your username.A small dropdown menu will appear — click on Two-Factor Authentication.A popup window will open. In the section, click .You will be redirected to a small window showing a QR code and a field below it to enter the TOTP. At this point, just use your phone with an authenticator app (such as Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator) to scan the code. Then, enter the 6-digit code that appears (it refreshes every 30 seconds) and click the button.After clicking the Continue button, you’ve successfully set up Two-Factor Authentication using the Authenticator App!Let’s now test if the authentication works correctly by logging out and logging back in again.Now, you may notice that previously, after entering a valid username and password, you were taken directly to the pgAdmin dashboard.However, after enabling Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) with an authenticator app, logging in with valid credentials (username and password) will now prompt you to enter your TOTP.Enter the 6-digit code from your authenticator app — just like you did during setup.If the TOTP is valid, clicking the Validate button will log you in and redirect you to the dashboard as usual.To enable Email-based OTP in pgAdmin 4, you need to configure an — this allows pgAdmin to send one-time codes to users via email.In this example, we use as the . Gmail does allow direct use of your account password for external apps. Instead, it allows you to generate a secure that acts as a special key for SMTP access.Once you’ve successfully created the App Password, you’ll get a 16-character code as shown below (this is what you’ll use in your config later).Once you’ve generated your Gmail App Password, just keep it somewhere safe — we’ll use it in the next step when configuring config_local.py.Let’s move on to setting up the configuration.In this section, we’re going to configure the config.py file. How you do this depends on your environment and preference.If you’re running pgAdmin inside a Debian- or Ubuntu-based container, you can use the CLI directly by running:docker exec -it <your_pgadmin_container_name> shInside the container, you should be able to use vi or vim to edit the file.However, since I’m using , I don’t have access to a full-featured shell inside the container.So I had to copy the file out, edit it on the host machine, and then copy it back into the container.config.py directly inside the container, feel free to skip the following steps.Just make sure the file includes the following parameters:MFA_SUPPORTED_METHODS = ["email", "authenticator"], as shown in the picture below.Accessing and copying files from the containerTo open a shell inside your pgAdmin container, run:docker exec -it <your_container_name> sh# in my case:docker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 shTo copy the config.py file from inside the container to your host machine, use:docker cp <your_container_name>:/<path_inside_container> <destination_on_host>/config.py# in my case:docker cp chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.py config.pyAfter successfully copying the file to your host machine, open it in your preferred text editor.The file is quite long, so scroll down and look for the following section:Two-Factor Authentication ConfigurationMake sure the following parameters are correctly set:MFA_ENABLED = TrueMFA_SUPPORTED_METHODS = ["email", "authenticator"]Once you’ve verified or updated the settings, copy the file back into the container:docker cp <local_file> <container_name>:<container_path>/config.py# in my case:docker cp config.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.pyOK, after we’ve finished configuring the config.py, let's move on to the next step — creating the config_local.py file.The config_local.py file is used to override values in config.py without disrupting the base configuration.Just like earlier, if your container has a shell editor like nano, vi, or vim, you can create and edit the file directly inside the container using:touch config_local.pyvi config_local.pyBut in my case, since I’m using Docker Desktop (and Alpine doesn’t include nano or vim by default, I believe), I'll create the file using Notepad on my host machine and save it with a .py extension.config_local.py file contains the following configuration:###########################################################################################MFA_ENABLED = TrueMFA_FORCE_REGISTRATION = False# Default: <APP_NAME> - Verification CodeMFA_EMAIL_SUBJECT = None###########################################################################################MAIL_SERVER = 'smtp.gmail.com'MAIL_USE_SSL = TrueMAIL_USERNAME = 'woraponasvn36@gmail.com'MAIL_PASSWORD = 'xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx'########################################################################################### → The SMTP server address for Gmail. This value is fixed and should not be changed. → Port used for SMTPS (SMTP over SSL). → This is Gmail’s default port for encrypted SSL connections. → Cannot be changed if MAIL_USE_SSL = True. → Enables SSL encryption for the connection. → Required when using port 465 with Gmail. → Your Gmail address that will be used to send OTP emails. → Must match the account used to generate the App Password. → The 16-digit App Password generated from your Gmail account. → Used instead of your actual Gmail login password.run docker cp config_local.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config_local.pyNow let’s check that config_local.py has been placed in the correct location.Run the following command to open a shell inside the container:docker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 shOK, now that config_local.py is in place inside the pgAdmin container, the only thing left to do is to apply the changes.Run the following command:sudo docker restart <your_container_id># In my case:Once restarted, access pgAdmin again via:http://<your_host_ip>:<pgadmin_port>Log in as usual. Then, at the top-right corner of the dashboard, click your account name and select Two-Factor Authentication.This time, we’ll proceed to set up Email-based authenticationAfter clicking the button, you’ll be redirected to a small window.In the field labeled “Enter the email address to send a code”, enter the email address where you want the OTP to be sent.Typically, this should match the MAIL_USERNAME you defined in your config_local.py file.After clicking the button, you’ll be redirected to a new pop-up window asking for a verification code.Check your email for the OTP that was just sent, enter the code, and click to complete the setup.This confirms that the email settings in config_local.py are working correctly — the OTP has been delivered.After clicking the button, a small pop-up will appear in the top-right corner, as shown in the image above, with the message:“Email Authentication registered successfully”At this point, you’ve successfully set up both TOTP and Email OTP for Two-Factor Authentication.Let’s now test that Email OTP is working properly by logging out and logging back in again.After setting up Email Authentication, you can now choose between both authentication methods, as shown in the following picture.Once you enter the OTP sent to your email, you’ll be successfully logged in to the pgAdmin dashboard.7.Summary of All Commandsdocker exec -it <your_pgadmin_container_name> shdocker exec -it <your_container_name> shdocker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 shdocker cp <your_container_name>:/<path_inside_container> <destination_on_host>/config.py# in my case:docker cp chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.py config.pydocker cp <local_file> <container_name>:<container_path>/config.pydocker cp config.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.pyvi config_local.pyrun docker cp config_local.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config_local.pydocker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 shsudo docker restart <your_container_id># In my case:Have a nice day!Every clap means a lot and motivates me to keep sharing.Love you all, readers. Bye-bye! 💙

item.title: UNDERSTANDING BASICS OF KUBERNETES
item.author: Naman Sharma
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes: The Basics ExplainedBefore delving into the concept of Kubernetes, let us first gain an understanding through a practical example, after which we will proceed with a detailed explanation.────────────────────────────────────────────────────────| Kubernetes Restaurant Cluster |────────────────────────────────────────────────────────| MASTER NODE || (Cook / Head Chef - Control Plane) || || ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ || | API Server (Restaurant Manager) | || | - Takes orders (kubectl commands) | || | - Communicates with all other components | || └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ || || ┌──────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────┐ || | Scheduler (Table Planner)| | Controller Manager | || | - Assigns tasks to | | - Ensures orders | || | available waiters | | stay active | || └──────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────┘ || || ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ || | etcd (Recipe Book) | || | - Stores menu, staff, and config data | || └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ||────────────────────────────────────────────────────────| WORKER NODES (Waiters) || || ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ || | Node 1 | | Node 2 | || | - Waiter executes orders | | - Another waiter | || | - Runs pods (customers) | | - More pods | || | | | | || | ┌────────────────────────┐ | | ┌──────────────┐ | || | | Pod (Customer) | | | | Pod | | || | | ┌────────────────────┐ | | | | ┌──────────┐ | | || | | | Container (Meal) | | | | | | Container| | | || | | └────────────────────┘ | | | └────────────┘ | | || | └────────────────────────┘ | └──────────────────┘ || └─────────────────────────────┘ |──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ||────────────────────────────────────────────────────────| Deployment = Menu Configuration || - Describes how many meals (replicas) to prepare || - Defines what ingredients (containers/images) to use|────────────────────────────────────────────────────────Kubernetes is the container orchestration tool which helps in managing the containers , scaling them , rollback , handling failure ,load balancing and recovering containers efficiently. Let’s assume we have five containers. Managing them manually especially during failures can be very difficult.For example, if one or more containers crash, you would have to restart them individually.solves this problem by automating container management. It can automatically detect failed containers and restart them, scale containers up or down based on load, and even perform rolling updates or rollbacks without downtime. It also provides and self-healing capabilities, making containerized applications much more resilient and easier to manage.cluster is a set of machines(virtual or physical) running Kubernetes, working together to run containerized applications.A (also called the ), which manages the cluster, schedules workloads, and makes decisions (like scaling, updates, etc.).One or more , which are responsible for actually running the applications.Inside , you have .Inside , you have one or more .What is a Pod in Kubernetes?A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It represents a single instance of a running process in your cluster.A pod consists of mainly one container can also consists of more than one.(multi-container pod)If there are more than one containers in the Pod, they share the same volume and network resources.What is the Namespace in Kubernetes?Logical separation inside our cluster to manage the resources.Used to separate environment such as prod, test and dev.By default in Kubernetes we have three namespace: default, kube-system and kube-public.Kubernetes Cluster Overview1. Master Node (Control Plane)Responsible for managing the Kubernetes cluster and maintaining the desired state.: Handles all REST API requests to the Kubernetes cluster. It’s the entry point for all administrative tasks.: A key-value store that stores the entire state of the cluster (e.g., pod states, configs).: Assigns Pods to Nodes based on resource availability and policies.1.4 kube-controller-manager: Watches the state of the cluster via the API server and makes changes to achieve the desired state (e.g., starting or stopping Pods).1.5 cloud-controller-manager: Integrates with cloud provider APIs (e.g., for load balancers, storage, node management).Runs the application workloads.: Communicates with the control plane, registers the node, and ensures containers in pods are running.: Manages network rules and handles routing of traffic to the appropriate pods.: The software responsible for running containers (e.g., Docker, containerd).2.4.1 Pod 1 → Container(s)2.4.2 Pod 2 → Container(s)A is an abstraction that defines a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them.It is used to expose the application internally or externally (e.g., ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer). is a tool that allows you to run on your personal machine for development and testing purposes.It creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster.This single node acts as both the Master Node and the Worker Node.We are using Minikube in this project to get a basic understanding of how Kubernetes works. Since setting up a full Kubernetes cluster requires more resources (like at least 2 CPUs an multiple nodes), Minikube provides a lightweight, single-node cluster that runs locally. It helps simulate a real Kubernetes environment for learning and testing without the need for complex infrastructure.Let us see how we can use Minikube locally.: If you are having a Linux operating system that's good. Install docker in it and minikube. If you are having windows operating system install WSL and Docker for windows. Clone an exisiting Project from Github or create your own.git clone https://github.com/namanupmanyu/DevopsApp.git#this is the sample project that I have created to understand complete Devops workflow. Start the minikube service. This will create a single-node Kubernetes cluster running inside a Docker container.minikube start --driver=docker#donot forget to start the docker service also.: Create a deployment.yml file which is already present in the project.minikube kubectl -- apply -f deployment.ymlminikube kubectl get pods#in the below mentioned screenshot i have used a command i.e. minikube kubectl delete deployment ruby-sample-deployment#used this beacuse i have already created a deployment.Step 5: Create a service.yml file which is already present in the project.minikube kubectl -- apply -f service.ymlminikube service <servicename>: Access your application.Note: Same steps we need to follow when we are using K8s.As we can see there is a little bug in our application and we need some changes. In my project, I have already create a readme.md file which tell us how to run the project. Let us make changes and apply them.: I have connect to the ec2 instance which was created using Terraform. Further We can see that Jenkins is already installed Docker is already installed this is because I have used ansible a configuration management tool. Let us now connect to the Jenkins on port 8080.Step 3: Lets us now make changes in our index.html page then after committing those change let us push the changes to the Github.cd devopsappcd app/views/homegit commit -m "commit message"#remeber to install git and create your own github account.Step 4: Let us now build our pipeline manually. To build automatically we have to add webhooks.(refer to the readme.md) Let us now access our application and see docker container is being created and is running or not.Step 6: Remember we were using minikube for deployment now let us repeat all the steps that I have mentioned earlier.minikube start --driver=dockerminikube kubectl -- apply -f deployment.ymlminikube kubectl get podsminikube kubectl -- apply -f service.ymlminikube service dev-app-serviceThat wraps up our understanding of the DevOps lifecycle for now. There are still a few parts we haven’t covered, and I’ll try to include them in my next blog.Thank you for reading…………

item.title: Cross-Cutting Concerns in .NET: Middleware, Filters, and Interceptors
item.author: Adem KORKMAZ

item.title: How to Deploy Amazon EKS Auto Mode with Custom Node Pools and Node classes Using Terraform
item.author: Manusha Pamoda
item.content:encoded: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) Auto Mode is a fully managed deployment option that streamlines running containerized applications at scale. It eliminates the operational overhead by handling infrastructure provisioning, cluster management, and performance optimization enabling developers to focus purely on application deployment and execution.In contrast to traditional EKS clusters, which require manual management of node groups and networking configurations, EKS Auto Mode simplifies the process by abstracting these complexities. The result is a seamless, cost-efficient, and secure Kubernetes experience.If we look at the EKS auto mode cluster architecture, we can see how much of change auto mode has implemented compare to previous EKS cluster architecture.Reasons to Use Custom Node Pools and Node ClassesWhen you create a cluster with EKS Auto Mode, it give you the option to use two built-in managed node pools — General Purpose and System — along with . While these built-in managed node pools simplify application deployment, they lack the flexibility needed for custom configurations.If you inspect the YAML configuration for the General Purpose node pool, you’ll notice that the instance families (c, m, r) are hardcoded into the setup:- key: eks.amazonaws.com/instance-categoryoperator: In- c- rWhile this default configuration works well for general workloads, it limits your ability to optimize for specific use cases.Why Choose Custom Node Pools?To leverage instance categories that are better suited for your applications, creating a custom node pool is the way to go. With custom node pools, you can select the instance types that best match your workload’s performance and cost requirements, without being restricted to the predefined categories.Additionally, although you can use the default node class with your custom node pool, EKS also allows you to create entirely new node classes, giving you even more control over how your workloads are managed and optimized.Terraform Setup for Amazon EKS in Auto Moderesource "aws_eks_cluster" "example" { name = "example" authentication_mode = "API" bootstrap_cluster_creator_admin_permissions = true } role_arn = aws_iam_role.cluster.arn version = "1.32" bootstrap_self_managed_addons = false enabled = true } kubernetes_network_config { elastic_load_balancing { } block_storage { } support_type = "STANDARD" endpoint_private_access = true endpoint_public_access = true aws_subnet.az1.id, aws_subnet.az3.id, } aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSClusterPolicy, aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSComputePolicy, aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy, aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy, aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy,}resource "aws_iam_role" "node" { name = "eks-auto-node-example" assume_role_policy = jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17" { Action = ["sts:AssumeRole"] Effect = "Allow" Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, })resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "node_AmazonEKSWorkerNodeMinimalPolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSWorkerNodeMinimalPolicy" role = aws_iam_role.node.name}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "node_AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPullOnly" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPullOnly" role = aws_iam_role.node.name}resource "aws_iam_role" "cluster" { name = "eks-cluster-example" assume_role_policy = jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17" { "sts:AssumeRole", ] Principal = { Service = "eks.amazonaws.com" } ]}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSClusterPolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSClusterPolicy" role = aws_iam_role.cluster.name}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSComputePolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSComputePolicy" role = aws_iam_role.cluster.name}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy" role = aws_iam_role.cluster.name}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy" role = aws_iam_role.cluster.name}resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy" { policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy" role = aws_iam_role.cluster.name}When using EKS Auto Mode these configurations are .compute_config.enabled = truekubernetes_network_config.elastic_load_balancing.enabled = truestorage_config.block_storage.enabled = truebootstrap_self_managed_addons = falseCreate Custom Node Pools and Node ClassesapiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1kind: NodePool name: custompool disruption: - nodes: 10% consolidationPolicy: WhenEmptyOrUnderutilized spec: nodeClassRef: kind: NodeClass requirements: - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type operator: In - on-demand - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type operator: In - "t3.medium" - key: kubernetes.io/arch operator: In - amd64 terminationGracePeriod: 24h0m0sapiVersion: eks.amazonaws.com/v1kind: NodeClass name: customclass ephemeralStorage: size: "80Gi" networkPolicy: DefaultAllow networkPolicyEventLogs: Disabled role: NonProd-EKS-auto-node-Role securityGroupSelectorTerms: - id: "sg-1" subnetSelectorTerms: - id: "subnet-2"In the template I have created a custom node pool with the t3.medium instance type. According to the AWS documentation, each instance type supports a specific maximum number of pods. For example, a t3.medium instance can host up to 15 pods.Key Considerations for Custom Node PoolsWhen configuring custom node pools in EKS, it’s crucial to understand the limitations of EKS Auto Mode. Specifically, the following labels are in Auto Mode which we are supported in the regular EKS mode:node.kubernetes.io/windows-buildkubernetes.io/osThis is because EKS Auto Mode exclusively supports Linux environments. It leverages , an open-source, Linux-based operating system purpose-built by AWS for running containers. Bottlerocket is optimized for security and minimalism, containing only the essential software required to efficiently run container workloads.By keeping these factors in mind, you can ensure your custom node pools are configured correctly and optimized for performance in EKS.AWS EKS Auto Mode simplifies Kubernetes infrastructure to the point where anyone without much prior experience in Kubernetes can deploy workloads. By abstracting away complex components EKS Auto Mode allows developers to focus more on building and deploying applications rather than managing the underlying infrastructure. As Kubernetes adoption continues to grow, this will be bring more users towards using Kubernetes.

item.title: 12 Bash One-Liners to Automate Your CI/CD Workflow
item.author: Obafemi

item.title: Build a Scalable Serverless API with AWS Console— Real Project Walkthrough
item.author: Mohamed Aymn
item.content:encoded: Build a Scalable Serverless API with AWS Console— Real Project Walkthrough💡 Read this article for free: Here’s the friend link — no login or membership required!In this guide, I’ll walk you through building a fully serverless backend for a shoe e-commerce website using AWS services:AWS Cognito for authenticationAPI Gateway for REST APIsLambda for serverless computeDynamoDB for NoSQL databasesServerless architecture offers several compelling advantages:: Focus on code, not infrastructure: Costs scale with your traffic: Handle traffic spikes with easeBuilt-in high availability: AWS ensures redundancy across zonesReal-World Example of How Serverless Benefits This Project?: You’d need to set up EC2 instances, load balancers, and databases, and manually scale them.: AWS handles scaling and infrastructure, allowing you to focus on API design, security, and UX.Here’s how our AWS components fit together:: Routes HTTP requests to specific Lambda functions: Runs backend logic (e.g., fetching shoe data, processing orders): Stores product details, user orders, and inventory: Manages user authentication and secure API accessCritical AWS Best Practices🚨 : Never use the root user for AWS projects. AWS follows the principle of least privilege, meaning you should only grant the minimum permissions needed for a task.📸 Screenshots are included below to help you set this up step by step!Step 1: Setting Up an IAM UserSkip this if you already have an IAM user with appropriate permissions.An IAM user is an entity in your AWS account representing a person or system interacting with AWS resources. Unlike the root user, an IAM user can have limited permissions, which is recommended for security. Learn more from the AWS docs.Sign in using the root user and head to the IAM consoleChoose “Users” and click “”3. For the , enter something like:YourName-IAM-Admin (e.g., Mohamed-IAM-Admin)“Provide user access to the AWS Management Console (optional)”If asked, “Are you providing access to a person?”, select “I want to create an IAM user.”5. Set console login credentials:Choose “Custom password.”Deselect “Users must create a new password at next sign-in.”6. Assign administrator permissions:Choose “Attach policies directly.”Select “AdministratorAccess”On the page, choose .From the list, select:Click , then .Now you’ve successfully created your IAM Admin user!7. Download credentials (optional):Stay on the User Created page.Click “Download .csv file” (it contains sign-in URL, username, and password)Copy the Console Sign-in URL for easy access later8. Log in as Your New IAM UserNow, you’re logged in as your IAM Admin User!Step 2: Setting Up Amazon Cognito User PoolWhat is Cognito and Why Use It?Cognito provides authentication, authorization, and user management for your applications. It’s like having “Sign in with Google” functionality but for your own app, completely free.Understanding Cognito Components:User pools (Authentication): Manages sign-up, sign-in, supports social logins, and provides JWT tokensIdentity pools (Authorization): Grants temporary AWS credentials for accessing AWS servicesUser Pools = Who can sign in? (Authentication) Identity Pools = What can they access? (Authorization)1. Head to the Cognito service from within the AWS Console2. Choose “User Pools” from the sidebar(Authentication service)3. Click “Create User Pool”4. Select “Traditional web application” and give your application a name5. Configure authentication settings:Choose sign-in identifiers (Email, Phone Number, Username)Select the required attributes for sign-upOptions for Sign-In IdentifiersIf only is selected, users must pick one as their .If a is included, users can sign in with .💡 Cannot be changed after app creation!Required Attributes for Sign-UpDetermines what users must provide during registration.Common required attributes: email, phone number, name, and address.If using a , an must also be required.We won’t need a return URL in our application for simplicity purposes, but if you’re curious about what a return URL is. Basically, it specifies where users will be directed after a successful authenticationAfter following along in creating user pools in Cognito. You should see that you have successfully created your user pool and access your sign-in page6. Complete the setup and test the sign-up/sign-in functionality7. Verify users in your user pool by checking Users under “User Management.”We have our user. Isn’t that amazing?Step 3: Creating a DynamoDB TableNow that users can sign up and sign in, we need a database to store user information:Go to the DynamoDB service2. Click “Create table” and give it a name (e.g., “Users”)3. Set the Partition key to “userId” with a string value type4. Keep the default settings and click “Create table.”Step 4: Creating a Lambda Function to Store UsersNow that we have our table, users can sign up successfully. We need a way to write our users to our users' table. And we are doing this through Lambda.lambda is code-as-a-service from awsWe need a way to write users to our DynamoDB table when they sign up:Head to Lambda’s dashboard and create a function2. Select “Author from scratch”3. Give your Lambda function a name (e.g., “StoreUsersInTable”)4. Select a runtime (Python 3.13 in this example)Now that we have created our lambda function successfully. Let’s write some code for this functionWriting the Lambda Function Code:import boto3import timedynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')table = dynamodb.Table('Users')def lambda_handler(event, context): user_attributes = event['request']['userAttributes'] 'userId': user_attributes['sub'], 'phone_number': user_attributes['phone_number'], 'email': user_attributes['email'], 'given_name': user_attributes['given_name'], 'family_name': user_attributes['family_name'], 'birthdate': user_attributes['birthdate'], 'gender': user_attributes['gender'], 'createdAt': int(time.time()) } table.put_item(Item=user_item) return event # Required for Cognito to proceed: Adjust the user attributes based on what you selected when creating your user pool.Why Return the Event in the Lambda Function?When Cognito triggers a (such as this one), it expects the function to return the event object unchanged. This is because:Cognito Relies on the Event FlowCognito passes an event object to Lambda, which contains user attributes and other metadata.If the function , Cognito assumes the process failed and may interrupt the user sign-up flow.2. Ensures a Smooth Sign-Up ProcessCognito from the Lambda function before continuing with the sign-up.If the response is missing or altered incorrectly, Cognito may reject the sign-up.3. Required for Post-Confirmation TriggersSince this function is a Post Confirmation Trigger, Cognito expects a return value to proceed with other internal processes (e.g., issuing tokens)TL; DR: Without returning event, Cognito wouldn’t know if the Lambda function , which could break the user registration process.Step 5: Setting Up Lambda PermissionsOur Lambda function needs permissions to access DynamoDB:Head to the Configuration tab of your Lambda functionClick on the role name to open the IAM roleGo to Policies in the IAM dashboardReplace the template with this JSON:{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "dynamodb:GetItem", "dynamodb:DeleteItem", "dynamodb:Query" "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:REGION:ACCOUNT_ID:table/Users" ]3. Replace REGION with your AWS region (e.g., us-east-1) and ACCOUNT_ID With your AWS Account ID4. Name the policy (e.g., “LambdaDynamoDBPolicy”) and save it5. Attach this policy to your Lambda execution roleStep 6: Connecting Cognito to LambdaNow we need to trigger our Lambda function when a user signs up:Go to Cognito and navigate to your user poolUnder “Extensions,” add a Lambda triggerSelect the trigger type as “Sign-up.”Choose the “Post confirmation trigger” optionSelect the Lambda function we created earliertest your application with a new user, and ,voila we have our user in our DynamoDB table as you seeStep 7: Setting Up API GatewayFinally, let’s create an API to interact with our backend:Go to the API Gateway in the AWS consoleClick “Create API” and select “REST API” (not REST API Private)Choose “New API” and give it a meaningful name (e.g., “ShoeStoreAPI”)For the API Endpoint type, select “Regional”for the API configurations. Select a new API option and give it a meaningful name (e.g., storeAPI)You can leave the description blank or write a few sentences about what you’re building.AWS API Gateway offers , each designed for different use cases:Understanding API Endpoint Types:Deployed within a specific AWS RegionBest for APIs used in the same region or fronted by CloudFrontIdeal for internal applications or regional services:Uses Amazon CloudFront to cache and optimize requests at edge locationsReduces latency for global usersBest for public APIs with worldwide usersAccessible only within a VPCNo direct internet accessIdeal for secure, internal APIs within an AWS environmentStep 8: Implementing API Gateway for Shoe Inventory ManagementNow that we have our authentication system and database set up, let’s create the actual API endpoints that will allow users to interact with our shoe inventory.Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy to create, publish, maintain, and secure APIs at any scale.in our project, it serves as the for all requests hitting our backend — from creating a new shoe listing to fetching or deleting one.We’ll use API Gateway to expose that trigger , which in turn interact with our database.We’ll create a REST API with the following endpoints:Each of these endpoints will be connected to a corresponding .On the “Resources” page, click “Create method.”Create each of the following routes:After doing these repetitive steps.Create Lambda Functions for Each Endpoint. For each endpoint, we need to create a corresponding Lambda function. Let’s start with the function to create a new shoe listing:Select “Author from scratch.”Name it “CreateShoeFunction”Select Python 3.13 for the runtimeHere’s an example code for the functionCreateShoe.import boto3import jsonfrom datetime import datetimedynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')table = dynamodb.Table('Shoes') # Make sure you've created this tabledef lambda_handler(event, context): try: # Parse the incoming JSON body body = json.loads(event['body']) # Check for required fields required_fields = ['name', 'brand', 'price', 'size'] for field in required_fields: if field not in body: 'statusCode': 400, 'body': json.dumps(f"Missing required field: {field}") } # Create shoe item with unique ID shoe_id = str(uuid.uuid4()) current_time = datetime.now().isoformat() 'ShoeId': shoe_id, 'brand': body['brand'], 'size': body['size'], 'description': body.get('description', ''), 'color': body.get('color', ''), 'in_stock': body.get('in_stock', True), 'created_at': current_time, 'updated_at': current_time } table.put_item(Item=shoe_item) # Return success response return { 'body': json.dumps({ 'message': 'Shoe created successfully', 'id': shoe_id, }) # Log the error and return error response print(f"Error creating shoe listing: {str(e)}") return { 'body': json.dumps(f"Error creating shoe listing: {str(e)}")Create similar Lambda functions for the other endpoints (GetAllShoes, GetShoeById, UpdateShoe, DeleteShoe, etc)2. Attach Lambda IntegrationsReturn to your API in API GatewayFor each route, click “Attach integration.”Choose the corresponding Lambda function for each route:POST /shoes → CreateShoeFunctionGET /shoes → GetAllShoesFunctionGET /shoes/{id} → GetShoeByIdFunctionPUT /shoes/{id} → UpdateShoeFunctionDELETE /shoes/{id} → DeleteShoeFunction3. Set Up Lambda Permissions. Each Lambda function needs permissions to access the DynamoDB table. Create a policy similar to the one we created earlier, but for the Shoes table:{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "dynamodb:GetItem", "dynamodb:DeleteItem", "dynamodb:Query" "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:REGION:ACCOUNT_ID:table/Shoes" ]Attach this policy to each Lambda function’s execution role.Creating the DynamoDB Table for ShoesBefore testing the API, make sure you’ve created a DynamoDB table for the shoe listings (Same steps as the table for users):Go to the DynamoDB consoleSet the table name to “Shoes”Set the partition key to “ShoeId” with type “String”Keep the default settingsStep 8: Testing Your API EndpointsLet’s first test our API directly from the AWS console to verify our setup:Navigate to your API in API GatewaySelect the endpoint you want to test (e.g., POST /shoes)For POST methods, enter a test payload like:{ "name": "Air Jordan 1", "price": 180, "color": "red", "description": "Classic basketball shoe"}Click "Test" and verify you receive a successful responseConsole testing is good for quick verification, but it doesn’t represent the true user experience since it bypasses authentication requirements.For a more realistic test, use tools like Postman, Insomnia, or cURL to test your API from outside AWS:Create a new shoe listing:curl -X POST https://your-api-url/shoes \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ "name": "Air Jordan 1", "price": 180, "color": "red", "description": "Classic basketball shoe" }'Step 9: Securing Your APIin a production environment, you’d have to secure your API connection. so that only verified users are allowed to create new listing for exampleUnderstanding API Testing ContextsBefore implementing authentication, it’s important to understand the difference between testing environments:Why You Needed to Generate a Token (via Cognito) in Thunder Client:When you're testing your API , like in Postman, you act like a real user or front-end client. If your API is secured with Cognito User Pools, then:Every request must include a (ID Token) in the Authorization header.But, When you test directly from the API Gateway console’s “Test” tab, you’re not really going through all the layers because:It bypasses Cognito authorizers and IAM checks. and It invokes the Lambda integration if you have the right permissions as the console user (you’re authenticated via AWS already). and checking integration responses — it doesn’t simulate secured access.So it works even for endpoints that require Cognito authentication — , not outside it.Implementing API Authorization with CognitoFollow these steps to secure your API with Amazon Cognito:Navigate to “Authorizers” from the API Gateway left sidebarAdd a new authorizer and select your previously created user pool Pay attention to capitalization in “Authorizer” fieldsRe-deploy your API after adding the authorizerNow if you try the same POST request from an external tool, you’ll receive a 401 Unauthorized error.Generating Authentication TokensTo make authenticated requests, you’ll need to:Create a user in your Cognito user poolVerify their email after signup3. Generate an authentication token using the AWS CLI:use the command line to run the following command:aws cognito-idp initiate-auth --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH --client-id 1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8 --auth-parameters USERNAME="40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656",PASSWORD="StrongPassword#1" Replace the client-id, username, and password with your specific values.Handling Secret Hash Requirementsif you get an error that the user pool was configured with a secret hash you’ll need to provide a secret hash. Generate it using this Python script:import hmacimport hashlibCLIENT_ID = "1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8"CLIENT_SECRET = "1u8s45nbbm9ejfqi6l0r2h13iie04s9k7n5unrleb3v2e8tcbvsl"USERNAME = "40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656"message = USERNAME + CLIENT_IDdigest = hmac.new(CLIENT_SECRET.encode(), message.encode(), hashlib.sha256).digest()secret_hash = base64.b64encode(digest).decode()# Output example: SoqY4XPRbMQ7sKNEglPRLhLQIw1vrY6txxpWs4gx5jc=Then modify your authentication command:aws cognito-idp initiate-auth --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH \ --client-id 1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8 \ --auth-parameters USERNAME="40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656",PASSWORD="StrongPassword#1",SECRET_HASH=SoqY4XPRbMQ7sKNEglPRLhLQIw1vrY6txxpWs4gx5jc=copy the ID Token, if you can’t see it in your console make sure to keep hitting enter and go back to your API testing platform ( I use thunder API an extension in VSCODE)⚠️ Important Security Warning ⚠️NEVER share your Cognito client ID and Secret in production environments. The credentials shown here are strictly for demonstration purposes. Exposing these credentials in real-world applications would cause a severe security breach.Making Authenticated RequestsAfter running the authentication command:Copy the ID Token from the responseAdd it to your API testing platform (like Thunder Client in VS Code)Include an “Authorization” header with the token valueMake your POST request againWith the proper authentication, you should now successfully create a shoe record securely.Congratulations! You’ve now built a complete serverless backend with:API Gateway for request handlingLambda functions for business logicDynamoDB for data storageCognito for user authentication and API securityRemember to delete your AWS resources when you’re done with this project to avoid unexpected charges.If you’re interested in expanding this project, you now have the understanding and capability to implement additional functions. And if you found the manual AWS console configuration tedious, check out my Terraform guide to learn how Infrastructure as Code can automate these processes much more efficiently. (Coming soon next week)

item.title: The Top Fell Off Australia's First Orbital-Class Rocket, Delaying Its Launch
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Australia's first orbital-class rocket launch was delayed after the nose cone of Gilmour Space's Eris rocket unexpectedly detached due to an electrical fault during final preparations. Although no damage occurred and no payload was onboard, the company is postponing the launch to investigate and replace the fairing before attempting another test flight. Ars Technica reports: Gilmour, the Australian startup that developed the Eris rocket, announced the setback in a post to the company's social media accounts Thursday. "During final launch preparations last night, an electrical fault triggered the system that opens the rocket's nose cone (the payload fairing)," Gilmour posted on LinkedIn. "This happened before any fuel was loaded into the vehicle. Most importantly, no one was injured, and early checks show no damage to the rocket or the launch pad."
Gilmour was gearing up for a launch attempt from a privately owned spaceport in the Australian state of Queensland early Friday, local time (Thursday in the United States). The company's Eris rocket, which was poised for its first test flight, stands about 82 feet (25 meters) tall with its payload fairing intact. It's designed to haul a payload of about 670 pounds (305 kilograms) to low-Earth orbit.
While Gilmour didn't release any photos of the accident, a company spokesperson confirmed to Ars that the payload fairing "deployed" after the unexpected electrical issue triggered the separation system. Payload fairings are like clamshells that enclose the satellites mounted to the top of their launch vehicle, protecting them from weather on the launch pad and from airflow as the rocket accelerates to supersonic speeds. Once in space, the rocket releases the payload shroud, usually in two halves. There were no satellites aboard the rocket as Gilmour prepared for its first test flight. The report notes that the Eris rocket is aiming to "become the first all-Australian launcher to reach orbit."

The version I released recently is a rewrite, and option -t no longer exists.To achieve a similar result with the new version of xorsearch.py, one uses now option -P (Python) and provides a Python function that filters out printable text: IsPrintableOption -D can then be used to dump the decoded data with an extra newline:Here too XOR encoding with key 0x6f reveals the hidden command.

item.title: Implementing a RISC-V Hypervisor
item.author: ingve
item.content:encoded:
To implement a seamless Linux integration into Starina, I decided to go with a Linux lightweight VM approach similar to WSL2. This means I need to implement a hypervisor that can run Linux.I had implemented an Intel VT-x based hypervisor before, but this time I wanted to try something different: RISC-V H-extension based hypervisor!This post is a diary of my journey of writing a RISC-V hypervisor incrementally.RISC-V H-extension introduces new CPU modes and some more CSRs (so-called control registers) to implement hardware-assisted virtualization. Its design is similar to Intel VT-x in the sense that both host and guest modes have their own kernel mode and user mode. This design makes it easy to run host OS along with guests, that is, guests behave as normal host processes (e.g. QEMU and Firecracker).How can I test a hypervisor on macOS?Unlike Linux KVM-based hypervisors (more specifically, virtual machine monitors), Starina is a new operating system and has been tested on QEMU.In this case, you typically need to use nested virtualization where the hardware-assisted virtualization is emulated by the host OS. That's how I did it for Intel VT-x.Here's great news: QEMU itself can emulate RISC-V H-extension! You just need to add to the QEMU command line. I presume this is thanks to RISC-V's simplicity and designers' foresight (and of course QEMU developers' effort!).Having a software emulation in QEMU is a key enabler when you're writing a new operating system from scratch because you can attach GDB to QEMU to debug the OS.Step 1: Entering the guestThe first thing to do is to enter the guest state. In RISC-V, guest kernel mode is called VS-mode. In RISC-V, you just fill a few CSRs. Specifically, should be set to 1 before instruction:The kernel panicked with an interesting error name: instruction -page fault. Yes, CPU has entered the guest mode!The next step is to run something in the guest mode. Let's start with a simple :To make it work, we need to prepare the guest's page table which maps the guest-physical address to the host-physical address so that the CPU can read the instructions in .RISC-V defines another paging modes called Sv39x4/Sv48x4/Sv57x4, and they're mostly identical to Sv39/Sv48/Sv57. The only caveat is U bit needs to be set to 1 for kernel pages too.Once is set, I got another trap reason:Step 3: Hello World from guest!Now we're ready to run a Hello World program. I wrote a simple program in assembly:.section .text .global _start
_start: li a0, 'H' li a7, 1 ecall
li a0, 'i' li a7, 1 ecall
li a0, '!' li a7, 1 ecall
li a0, '\n' li a7, 1 ecall
unimp This assumes hypervisor's ecall handler implements the SBI, a RISC-V's BIOS interface. This snippet calls so-called API and finally calls an invalid instruction () to trigger a trap.Building this tiny guest OS is easy:$ clang --target=riscv64 -march=rv64g -nostdlib -Wl,-Ttext=0x80200000 guest.S -o guest.elf $ llvm-objcopy -O binary guest.elf guest.bin Our minimal guest Hello World worked, so it's time to try with Linux.Here are some kernel config options I enabled:CONFIG_SERIAL_EARLYCON_RISCV_SBI=y CONFIG_RISCV_SBI_V01=y CONFIG_HVC_RISCV_SBI=y CONFIG_RISCV_TIMER=y Linux kernel for RISC-V can be built with:make ARCH=riscv CROSS_COMPILE=riscv64-linux-gnu- Image The boot image format is documented here, and it's basically raw binary. Just copy the image file into the guest memory, and jump to the beginning when the CPU enters the guest mode.Looks like Linux booted, but it crashed with a null dereference:According to the value, the kernel panicked at :$ gobjdump -d linux/vmlinux | grep -A 5 801cace8 | head ffffffff801cace8 <__pi_fdt32_ld>: ffffffff801cace8: 00054783 lbu a5,0(a0) <-- null deferecence here! ffffffff801cacec: 00154703 lbu a4,1(a0) ffffffff801cacf0: 0187979b slliw a5,a5,0x18 In RISC-V, we need to provide a device tree, a tree data structure defining what devices are available in the computer.Fortunately, we can use a Rust crate for this: vm-fdt, a device tree builder from RustVMM project (famous for Firecracker). The library supports no_std, so it was super easy to use it in Starina (thank you, thank you RustVMM folks!).To make Linux work, the device tree should include at least: free memory areas ( in ), and CPUs with RISC-V ISA extensions ().After adding the device tree, I got another trap. Looks like Linux tried to read but failed because was not set:hcounteren register is clear, attempts to read the cycle, time, instret, or hpmcounter n register while V=1 will cause a virtual-instruction exceptionThe fix is to fill CSR. Easy peasy.While booting, Linux kernel tries to probe the abilities of the CPU and peripherals. That intialization step is mostly done by itself without any help from the hypervisor. However, there's a bit head-scratching part: the timer speed detection.In the step, Linux kernel waits for to progress and it looks as if it's hanging if the hypervisor doesn't implement timer.In RISC-V there are two approaches to implement a timer: Call the hypervisor (SBI) to set the timer. A CPU extension to trigger a timer interrupt without hypervisor's help.This step is also the first time I needed to inject interrupts into the guest. While RISC-V spec is clean, but I was totally lost with how to do it. Setting was what I needed to do, but I was confused by RISC-V Advanced Interrupt Architecture, which extends the RISC-V interrupt handling.RISC-V extensions are more somtimes look like "patches" to the base spec. RISC-V Unified Database is a great resource to understand how the extensions extend the base spec.Once I enabled extension and implemented interrupt handling correctly, the kernel booted successfully:Linux booted successfully on Starina's hypervisor. The next step is to provide devices. In RISC-V, as in other architectures, devices are mapped to physical addresses (memory-mapped I/O). Devices need to be emulated are: The guest kernel needs it to read pending interrupts, and acknowledge them. PLIC in RISC-V. Where the root filesystem is stored. Virtio-blk is a popular choice. For networking. Virtio-net is the most popular choice.In hypervisor, MMIO is implemented by mapping anything at the address range. That is, everytime the guest tries to access the address range:The CPU tries to read/write a MMIO address, but it's not mapped. Trigger a guest page fault.The hypervisor checks if the address range is a MMIO address.Decode the instruction to determine destination/source registers, and the access width.Emulate the MMIO operation.If it's a read, write the value to the register.Advance the program counter to the next instruction, and resume the guest.Doesn't it sound a bit hacky? In MMIO, hypervisor behaves like a CPU interpreter. Fortunately, RISC-V is very simple and gives us summary of the instruction in CSR. In x86-64, ... good luck!For future RISC-V hypervisor developers, here are few pitfalls I encountered:A guest physical address written to is shifted right by 2 bits. You'll see a weird address if you forget this. could be compressed instruction. This means the instruction width is not always 4 bytes, but sometimes 2 bytes.This is the last step of this post. While virito-blk is a typical choise to provide the root filesystem, I chose something different: virtio-fs.Why? Because it allows more seamless integration with Starina. I plan to write another post about the integration, but in short, Starina provides some virtual files to the guest.Virtio-fs is a virtual filesystem over Virtio. More specifically, it uses FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) protocol to communicate with the guest kernel.I have nothing to say about this step because we've finished the hypervisor-specific steps already! Design a good virtio library, implement the virtio-fs emulation on top of it, and you're done:Tip: Debugging both hypervisor/guest worldsStarina supports Unikernel-like mode, where the microkernel and apps are built into a single ELF executable. This is not only for performance, but also for debugging.Here's a script which enabled me to watch VMM, hypervisor in Starina kernel, and the Linux kernel in guest:# Load Starina (hypervisor's) debug info file build/kernel/debug/kernel
# Load Linux (guest's) debug info add-symbol-file path/to/vmlinux And look! You're seeing the guest's kernel stack trace!This is why I haven't yet implemented stack traces in Starina: you just need to attach the GDB and type .

item.title: NASA Resurrects Voyager 1 Interstellar Spacecraft's Thrusters After 20 Years
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
NASA engineers have successfully revived Voyager 1's backup thrusters, unused since 2004 and once considered defunct. Space.com reports: This remarkable feat became necessary because the spacecraft's primary thrusters, which control its orientation, have been degrading due to residue buildup. If its thrusters fail completely, Voyager 1 could lose its ability to point its antenna toward Earth, therefore cutting off communication with Earth after nearly 50 years of operation. To make matters more urgent, the team faced a strict deadline while trying to remedy the thruster situation. After May 4, the Earth-based antenna that sends commands to Voyager 1 -- and its twin, Voyager 2 -- was scheduled to go offline for months of upgrades. This would have made timely intervention impossible.
To solve the problem, NASA's team had to reactivate Voyager 1's long-dormant backup roll thrusters and then attempt to restart the heaters that keep them operational. If the star tracker drifted too far from its guide star during this process, the roll thrusters would automatically fire as a safety measure -- but if the heaters weren't back online by then, firing the thrusters could cause a dangerous pressure spike. So, the team had to precisely realign the star tracker before the thrusters engaged. Because Voyager is so incredibly distant, the team faced an agonizing 23-hour wait for the radio signal to travel all the way back to Earth. If the test had failed, Voyager might have already been in serious trouble. Then, on March 20, their patience was finally rewarded when Voyager responded perfectly to their commands. Within 20 minutes of receiving the signal, the team saw the thruster heaters' temperature soar -- a clear sign that the backup thrusters were firing as planned. "It was such a glorious moment. Team morale was very high that day," Todd Barber, the mission's propulsion lead at JPL, said in the statement. "These thrusters were considered dead. And that was a legitimate conclusion. It's just that one of our engineers had this insight that maybe there was this other possible cause, and it was fixable. It was yet another miracle save for Voyager."

item.title: JavaScript's New Superpower: Explicit Resource Management
item.author: olalonde
item.content:encoded: The Explicit Resource Management proposal introduces a deterministic approach to explicitly manage the lifecycle of resources like file handles, network connections, and more. This proposal brings the following additions to the language: the and declarations, which automatically calls dispose method when a resource goes out of scope; and symbols for cleanup operations; two new global objects and as containers to aggregate disposable resources; and as a new type of error (contain both the error that was most recently thrown, as well as the error that was suppressed) to address the scenario where an error occurs during the disposal of a resource, and potientially masking an existing error thrown from the body, or from the disposal of another resource. These additions enable developers to write more robust, performant, and maintainable code by providing fine-grained control over resource disposal. and declarations #The core of the Explicit Resource Management proposal lies in the and declarations. The declaration is designed for synchronous resources, ensuring that the method of a disposable resource is called when the scope in which it's declared exits. For asynchronous resources, the declaration works similarly, but ensures that the method is called and the result of this calling is awaited, allowing for asynchronous cleanup operations. This distinction enables developers to reliably manage both synchronous and asynchronous resources, preventing leaks and improving overall code quality. The and keywords can be used inside braces (such as blocks, for loops and function bodies), and cannot be used in top-levels.For example, when working with ReadableStreamDefaultReader, it's crucial to call to unlock the stream and allow it to be used elsewhere. However, error handling introduces a common problem: if an error occurs during the reading process, and you forget to call before the error propagates, the stream remains locked. Let's start with a naive example: responsePromise responsePromise responsePromise url response responsePromiseresponseok processedData response reader responsebody done value processedDatadone done value readervalue reader processedDataSo it is crucial for developers to have block while using streams and put in . This pattern ensures that is always called. reader responsebody done value processedDatadone done value readervalue reader processedDataAn alternative to write this code is to create a disposable object , which has the reader (response.body.getReader()) and the method that calls this.reader.releaseLock(). The declaration ensures that readerResource[Symbol.dispose]() is called when the code block exits, and remembering to call is no longer needed because the using declaration handles it. Integration of and in web APIs like streams may happen in the future, so developers do not have to write the manual wrapper object. reader responsebody done value using readerResource responsebodySymboldisposereader reader readerResource done value processedDatadone done value readervalue processedData and #To further facilitate managing multiple disposable resources, the proposal introduces and . These stack-based structures allow developers to group and dispose of multiple resources in a coordinated manner. Resources are added to the stack, and when the stack is disposed, either synchronously or asynchronously, the resources are disposed of in the reverse order they were added, ensuring that any dependencies between them are handled correctly. This simplifies the cleanup process when dealing with complex scenarios involving multiple related resources. Both structures provide methods like , , and to add resources or disposal actions, and a or method to trigger the cleanup. and have and , respectively, so they can be used with and keywords. They offer a robust way to manage the disposal of multiple resources within a defined scope.Let’s take a look at each method and see an example of it: adds a resource to the top of the stack. readerResource responsebodySymboldisposereader console using stack stackreaderResource adds a non-disposable resource and a disposal callback to the top of the stack. using stack stack responsebody reader reader console adds a disposal callback to the top of the stack. It's useful for adding cleanup actions that don't have an associated resource. using stack stack console moves all resources currently in this stack into a new . This can be useful if you need to transfer ownership of resources to another part of your code. using stack stack responsebody reader reader console using newStack stack in DisposableStack and in AsyncDisposableStack dispose the resources within this object. readerResource responsebodySymboldisposereader console stack stackreaderResource stackExplicit Resource Management is shipped in Chromium 134 and V8 v13.8.Explicit Resource Management support #

item.title: Rockchip Open-Source NPU Driver "RKNN" Continues Making Progress
item.author: Michael Larabel
item.content:encoded: Started just over one year ago was the effort to create an open-source Rockchip NPU driver. While not yet mainlined to the Linux kernel, progress on this kernel driver and associated user-space driver continues...

item.title: CNCF Live Webinar: Kubernetes 1.33 Release
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded: Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

item.title: Build, don’t bind: Accel’s Sonali De Rycker on Europe’s AI crossroads
item.author: Connie Loizos
item.content:encoded: Sonali De Rycker, a general partner at Accel and one of Europe’s most influential venture capitalists, is bullish about the continent’s prospects in AI. But she’s wary of regulatory overreach that could hamstring its momentum. At a TechCrunch StrictlyVC evening earlier this week in London, De Rycker reflected on Europe’s place in the global AI […]

item.title: Intel ISPC 1.27 Released With AVX10.2 Support
item.author: Michael Larabel
item.content:encoded: Intel's Implicit SPMD Program Compiler (ISPC) is out with a new feature release in preparing for AVX10.2-enabled processors and bringing other enhancements to this C programming language variant focused on single program, multiple data (SPMD) programming...

item.title: FDA Clears First Blood Test To Help Diagnose Alzheimer's Disease
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from the Associated Press: U.S. health officials on Friday endorsed the first blood test that can help diagnose Alzheimer's and identify patients who may benefit from drugs that can modestly slow the memory-destroying disease. The test can aid doctors in determining whether a patient's memory problems are due to Alzheimer's or a number of other medical conditions that can cause cognitive difficulties. The Food and Drug Administration cleared it for patients 55 and older who are showing early signs of the disease.
The new test, from Fujirebio Diagnostics, Inc., identifies a sticky brain plaque, known as beta-amyloid, that is a key marker for Alzheimer's. Previously, the only FDA-approved methods for detecting amyloid were invasive tests of spinal fluid or expensive PET scans. The lower costs and convenience of a blood test could also help expand use of two new drugs, Leqembi and Kisunla, which have been shown to slightly slow the progression of Alzheimer's by clearing amyloid from the brain. Doctors are required to test patients for the plaque before prescribing the drugs, which require regular IV infusions. [...]
A number of specialty hospitals and laboratories have already developed their own in-house tests for amyloid in recent years. But those tests aren't reviewed by the FDA and generally aren't covered by insurance. Doctors have also had little data to judge which tests are reliable and accurate, leading to an unregulated marketplace that some have called a "wild west." Several larger diagnostic and drug companies are also developing their own tests for FDA approval, including Roche, Eli Lilly and C2N Diagnostics. The tests can only be ordered by a doctor and aren't intended for people who don't yet have any symptoms.

item.title: A Linux kernel developer plays with Home Assistant: general impressions
item.author: pabs3
item.content:encoded:
By May 9, 2025 Those of us who have spent our lives playing with computers naturally see the appeal of deploying them though the home for both data acquisition and automation. But many of us who have watched the evolution of the technology industry are increasingly unwilling to entrust critical household functions to cloud-based servers run by companies that may not have our best interests at heart. The Apache-licensed Home Assistant project offers a welcome alternative: locally controlled automation with free software. This two-part series covers roughly a year of Home Assistant use, starting with a set of overall observations about the project.
This is not the first time that LWN has looked at this project, of course; this review gives a snapshot of what Home Assistant looked like five years ago, while this 2023 article gives a good overview of the project's history, governance, and overall direction. I will endeavor to not duplicate that material here.
At a first glance, Home Assistant bears some of the hallmarks of a company-owned project. The company in question, Nabu Casa, was formed around the project and employs a number of its key developers. One of the ways in which the company makes money is with a $65/year subscription service, providing remote access to Home Assistant servers installed on firewalled residential networks. Home Assistant has support for that remote option, and no others. It would be interesting to see what would happen to a pull request adding support for, say, OpenThings Cloud as an alternative. The fate of that request would say a lot about how open the project really is.
This LWN.net subscription-only content has been made available to you by an LWN subscriber. To see more of this content, please take advantage of the following special offer.
(For the record, I have bought the Nabu Casa subscription rather than, say, using WireGuard to make a port available on an accessible system; it is a hassle-free way to solve the problem and support the development of this software).
That said, most of the warning signs that accompany a corporate-controlled project are not present with Home Assistant. The project's contributor license agreement is a derivative of the kernel's developer certificate of origin; contributors retain their copyright on their work. Since the 2024.4 release, the Home Assistant core repository has acquired over 17,000 changesets from over 900 contributors. While a number of Nabu Casa employees (helpfully listed on this page) appear in the top ten contributors, they do not dominate that list.
Home Assistant is clearly an active project with a wide developer base. In 2024, overall responsibility for this project was transferred to the newly created Open Home Foundation. This project is probably here to stay, and seems unlikely to take a hostile turn in the future. For a system that sits at the core of one's home, those are important characteristics.
Linux users tend to be somewhat spoiled; installing a new application is typically a matter of a single package-manager command. Home Assistant does not really fit into that model. The first three options on the installation page involve dedicated computers — two of which are sold by Nabu Casa. For those wanting to install it on a general-purpose computer, the recommended course is to install the Home Assistant Operating System, a bespoke Linux distribution that runs Home Assistant within a Docker container. There is also a container-based method that can run on another distribution, but this installation does not support the add-ons feature.
Home Assistant, in other words, is not really set up to be just another application on a Linux system. If one scrolls far enough, though, one will find, the instructions to install onto a "normal" Linux system, suitably guarded with warnings about how it is an "" method. Of course, that is what I did, putting the software onto an existing system running Fedora. The whole thing subsequently broke when a distribution upgrade replaced Python, but that was easily enough repaired. As a whole, the installation has worked as expected.
Out of the box, though, a new Home Assistant installation does not do much. Its job, after all, is to interface with the systems throughout the house, and every house is different. While Home Assistant can find some systems automatically (it found the Brother printer and dutifully informed me that the device was, inevitably, low on cyan toner), it usually needs to be told about what is installed in the house. Thus, the user quickly delves into the world of "integrations" — the device drivers of Home Assistant.
For each remotely accessible device in the house, there is, hopefully, at least one integration available that allows Home Assistant to work with it. Many integrations are packaged with the system itself, and can be found by way of a simple search screen in the Home Assistant web interface. A much larger set is packaged separately, usually in the Home Assistant Community Store, or HACS; it is fair to say that most users will end up getting at least some integrations from this source. Setting up HACS requires a few steps and, unfortunately, requires the user to have a GitHub account for full integration. It possible to install HACS integrations without that account, but it is a manual process that loses support for features like update tracking.
Most integrations, at setup time, will discover any of the appropriate devices on the network — if those devices support that sort of discovery, of course. Often, using an integration will require the credentials to log into the cloud account provided by the vendor of the devices in question. When possible, integrations mostly strive to operate entirely locally; some only use the cloud connection for the initial device discovery. When there is no alternative, though, integrations will remain logged into the cloud account and interact with their devices that way; this mode may or may not be supported (or condoned) by the vendor. There are, of course, some vendors that are actively hostile to integration with Home Assistant.
As might be expected, the quality of integrations varies widely. Most of the integrations I have tried have worked well enough. The OpenSprinkler (reviewed here in 2023) integration, instead, thoroughly corrupted the device configuration, exposing me to the shame of being seen with a less-than-perfect lawn; it was quickly removed. It is an especially nice surprise when a device comes with Home Assistant support provided by the vendor, but that is still a relatively rare occurrence. Home Assistant now is in a position similar to Linux 25 years ago; many devices are supported, but often in spite of their vendor, and one has to choose components carefully.
Home Assistant sits at the core of the home network; it has access to sensors that can reveal a lot about the occupants of the home, and it collects data in a single location. An installation will be exposed to the Internet if its owner needs remote access. There is clearly potential for a security disaster here.
The project has a posted security policy describing the project's stance; it asks for a 90-day embargo on the reporting of any security issues. Authors writing about the project's security are encouraged to run their work past the project "so we can ensure that all claims are correct". The security policy explicitly excludes reports regarding third-party integrations (the core project cannot fix those, after all). The project is also uninterested in any sort of privilege escalation by users who are logged into Home Assistant, assuming that anybody who has an account is fully trusted.
There is no overall vetting of third-party integrations, which are, in the end, just more Python code. So loading an unknown integration is similar to importing an unknown module from PyPI; it will probably work, but the potential for trouble is there. The project has occasionally reported security problems in third-party integrations, but such reports are rare. I am unable to find any reports of actively malicious integrations in the wild, but one seems destined to appear sooner or later. Actually doing something with Home Assistant The first step for the owner of a new Home Assistant installation is, naturally, to seek out integrations for the devices installed in the home. On successful installation and initialization, an integration will add one or more "devices" to the system, each of which has some number of "sensors" for data it reports, and possible "controls" to change its operating state. A heat-pump head, for example, may have sensors for the current temperature and humidity, and controls for its operating mode, fan speed, vane direction, and more.
It is worth noting that the setup of these entities seems a bit non-deterministic at times. My solar system has 22 panels with inverters, each of which reports nearly a dozen parameters (voltage, current, frequency, temperature, etc.). There is no easy way to determine which panel is reporting, for example, , especially since almost certainly corresponds to a panel. My experience is that Home Assistant is a system for people who are willing to spend a lot of time fiddling around with things to get them to a working state. Dealing with these sensors was an early introduction to that; it took some time to figure out the mapping between names and rooftop positions, then to rename each sensor to something more helpful.
The next level of fiddling around is setting up dashboards. Home Assistant offers a great deal of flexibility in the information and controls it provides to the user; it is possible to set up screens focused on, say, energy production or climate control. Happily, the days when this configuration had to be done by writing YAML snippets are mostly in the past at this point; one occasionally still has to dip into YAML, but it does not happen often. The interface is not always intuitive, but it is fairly slick, interactive, and functional.
Another part of Home Assistant that I have not yet played with much is automations and scenes. Automations are simple rule-triggered programs that make changes to some controls. They can carry out actions like "turn on the front light when it gets dark" or "play scary music if somebody rings the doorbell and nobody is home". Scenes are sets of canned device configurations. One might create a scene called "in-laws visiting" that plays loud punk music, sets the temperature to just above freezing, disables all voice control, and tunes all of the light bulbs to 6000K, for example.
The good news is that, unless the fiddling itself is the point (and it can be a good one), there comes a time when things just work and the fiddling can stop. A well-configured Home Assistant instance provides detailed information about the state of the home — and control where the devices allow it — to any web browser that can reach it and log in. There are (open-source) apps that bring this support to mobile devices in a way that is nearly indistinguishable from how the web interface works.
All told, it is clear why Home Assistant has a strong and growing following. It is an open platform that brings control to an industry that is doing its best to keep a firm grasp on our homes and the data they create. Home Assistant shows that we can do nicely without all of these fragile, non-interoperable, rug-pull-susceptible cloud systems. Just like Linux proved that we can have control over our computers, Home Assistant shows that we do not have to surrender control over our homes.
This article has gotten long, and is remarkably short on interesting things that one can actually with Home Assistant. There are some interesting stories to be told along those lines; they will appear shortly in the second, concluding part of this series.

item.title: Buc-ee’s Gas Station Chain Is Becoming The Monster Energy Of Gas Stations For Trademark Bullying
item.author: Dark Helmet
item.content:encoded: If you’re from Texas or its surrounding states, you’ve probably heard of Buc-ee’s. The gas station and convenience store chain is a staple of the area and has a well-known branded logo that looks like this.I recently came across a settlement that was the result of a trademark suit Buc-ee’s initiated back in November against Duckees Drive Thru, which is a liquor store in Missouri. The details of the settlement were, as frustratingly usual, not made public. But I would have to damned well hope the settlement wasn’t too much in favor of Buc-ee’s given the details of the case. Liquor stores aren’t gas stations, Buc-ee’s and Duckees aren’t actually all that similar thanks to the distinctive dash in the former’s name, and you couldn’t possibly get me high or drunk enough to make me think this logo looks confusingly similar to Buc-ee’s. Unless Missouri beavers look different than what I’m used to, that’s a duck. The only settlement that should have occurred would be for Buc-ee’s to “settle” for being told to “duck” all the way off.It turns out this isn’t some one-off. Apparently anytime Buc-ee’s sees another company out there in even a remotely tangential industry that has an animal cartoon with a yellow circle behind it for a logo, they go full feral beaver over it. There are many, many examples of this.There is the active suit right now between Buc-ee’s and Choke Canyon, a barbeque joint, with the former arguing this looks too much like a beaver.Thank god Steve Irwin isn’t alive to see that one.How about this one, which also ended in a settlement, for a chicken joint?During the case, which Chicks settled out of court, Buc-ee’s brazenly accused the store of improper use of: “friendly smiling cartoon animals,” “the use of a black circle,” “the use of a yellow background,” “the use of the red-colored cockscomb on the heads of the chickens,” “the prominent use of sharply drawn black edges,” and “the use of six letters in raised and lowered block font in the name.”Or how about Irv’s, a restaurant that doesn’t even feature a non-human animal?“Defendants’ blatant copying of the Beaver Logo shows that Defendants’ actions are willful, knowing, and committed with the intent of benefiting from the goodwill and concept of Buc-ee’s…,” the complaint against Irv’s states, before offering a photo comparison (included below).“From a distance it is difficult to discern the logo from the logo of Buc-ee’s.”And yet that one also ended in a settlement.To be clear, this is pure trademark bullying bullshit. Buc-ee’s is big and these others are small and so they cave. And since nobody in the legal and court systems are willing or capable of doing anything about it, the bullying continues unabated. In a just world, Buc-ee’s would be sanctioned for even wasting the court’s and its victims’ time, money, and energy with this bullshit.But in lieu of justice, we can at least highlight the company’s bullying ways for the public.
